definition: Represent each image or ROI by normalized intensity histograms, quantitative radiomics features, or feature-domain summaries, then compute histogram or embedding similarity. This audits whether image-model performance depends on scanner/protocol-induced image distribution overlap.
example_usage: python histogram_js_similarity.py --train train_images.csv --eval eval_images.csv --out pairwise_similarity.csv --hist-cols-prefix hist_

citations.title: Making Radiomics More Reproducible across Scanner and Imaging Protocol Variations: A Review of Harmonization Methods

evidence.summary: The review explains that radiomic features vary across scanners, acquisition protocols, and reconstruction settings.
The paper discusses standardization and harmonization across scanner/protocol variation.

citations.title: Characterization of Diffusion Metric Map Similarity in Data From a Clinical Data Repository Using Histogram Distances

evidence.summary: The paper uses histogram distance metrics to characterize similarity of MRI-derived metric maps across acquisition settings.
Histogram distance is proposed as a practical way to determine whether repository images are sufficiently similar for reuse.

limitations: Histogram similarity ignores spatial organization unless radiomics or texture features are included.
ROI segmentation, binning, and normalization choices can dominate the result.
Image feature similarity can conflate disease phenotype with scanner/protocol effects.
quality_gates: Report binning, intensity normalization, ROI definition, and radiomics extractor version.
Run scanner/protocol metadata similarity alongside feature-distribution similarity.
Check whether low-similarity bins are enriched for site, scanner, or label prevalence.
